blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e.,  $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following relation:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This equation expresses the total differential of a function in terms of its partial derivatives.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$, $y$, and $z$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: This figure illustrates the relationship between points in the $xy$-plane and their corresponding changes in the $z$-dimension, emphasizing how position on the surface correlates with changes in output value.

blocks.text:
Thus, 

total change in $z$ = change in $z$ due to the change in $x$ + change in $z$ due to the change in $y$.

The change in $z$ over time may be measured by the product of the change in $x$ multiplied by the rate at which $z$ is changing with respect to $x$; and this fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21).

Let P be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P on the surface. The change of position is most easily understood if we also look at the change in $z$ which occurs from point $P$ to point $Q$ on the surface (z being kept constant).
